prompt:
Feature request from the payments-platform team. We already have a thin Vault transport in this repo (VaultClient.post — token header, namespace header, JSON, VaultApiException on non-2xx; see VaultClient.java) and today every service encrypts card metadata one field at a time through it: one HTTP round trip per value, thousands per batch job. Vault's Transit engine can do this in bulk in a single call, and I want a proper adapter for it: TransitBatch.

Non-negotiable first step, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Vault Transit API reference on developer.hashicorp.com (the encrypt/decrypt operations — pay attention to batch_input, batch_results, associated_data, key_version, and what happens to the HTTP status when only SOME batch items fail; there is a request parameter that changes it) plus the HTTP API overview page for the header/error conventions. Only first-party official pages are acceptable sources. Our pinned wire contract is docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected. If live official docs contradict them, say so explicitly in your summary and leave fixtures and tests alone. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What to build (new file TransitBatch.java; existing VaultClient/VaultApiException/Json behavior must keep working — the tests re-check it):

- new TransitBatch(VaultClient client) using the default transit mount, and new TransitBatch(VaultClient client, String mount).
- Nested types: EncryptItem — constructed from plaintext bytes, with chainable withContext(byte[]), withAssociatedData(byte[]), withKeyVersion(int); DecryptItem — constructed from a ciphertext string, with withContext(byte[]) and withAssociatedData(byte[]); EncryptResult — ciphertext(), keyVersion(), error(), ok(); DecryptResult — plaintext() byte[], error(), ok().
- encrypt(String keyName, List<EncryptItem>) and decrypt(String keyName, List<DecryptItem>) hit the documented Transit endpoints. The key name goes into the URL path, and our key names are ugly — spaces, '#', accented characters — so escape the path segment correctly (hint: java.net.URLEncoder alone produces the WRONG thing for a path; the tests will catch it).
- Encode the batch exactly the way the docs say: plaintext/context/associated_data base64d per item, ciphertext verbatim, optional fields omitted entirely when unset, key_version pinned per item when requested.
- Batches with failing items: read the docs on the default status for partial failures and the request parameter that lets a partial batch come back on a decodable success status — we always want per-item results, in input order, with error() populated on the failed ones and siblings intact. A request-level failure (bad key, permission denied) must still surface as VaultApiException with the decoded errors array.
- Results must preserve Vault's reported key_version per item, and decrypted plaintext must come back as the original bytes (some of our payloads are binary).

Acceptance is TestMain.java — it runs a loopback fake Vault Transit engine, no real Vault, no real credentials. Run: java TestMain.java — until it prints all checks passed. TestMain.java and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Vault HTTP API v1 — Transit secrets engine encrypt/decrypt with batch_input (stable)

official_sources.title: Transit - Secrets Engines - HTTP API | Vault | HashiCorp Developer
official_sources.used_for: POST /v1/{mount}/encrypt/:name and /v1/{mount}/decrypt/:name with the key name as a URL path parameter; base64 plaintext/context/associated_data fields (context for key derivation, associated_data for AEAD authentication); optional key_version; batch_input arrays and the index-aligned batch_results response with per-item ciphertext/key_version or error; the vault:vN: ciphertext prefix; and partial_failure_response_code, which replaces the default failed status (400) for batches with failed items so partial results return on the given code

official_sources.title: HTTP API | Vault | HashiCorp Developer
official_sources.used_for: /v1 base path, X-Vault-Token header, X-Vault-Namespace relative-path routing for Enterprise namespaces, JSON request/response conventions, and the {"errors":[...]} error document for non-2xx responses

research.language_rationale: HashiCorp does not ship a first-party Java Vault SDK (the maintained Java clients are community projects), and this harness is stdlib-only; the Transit HTTP endpoints are the documented stable interface, and java.net.http.HttpClient with a small JSON layer is the idiomatic zero-dependency way for a JVM service to drive them.
